hypotheses_results.hypothesis: H1: A 4-day work week does not decrease total weekly output
hypotheses_results.explanation: Mean weekly output changed from 47.2 to 46.8 units, a negligible 0.8% decrease. A two-sample t-test shows this difference is not statistically significant (p > 0.05), supporting the hypothesis that productivity was maintained.

hypotheses_results.hypothesis: H2: Employee satisfaction scores increase with the 4-day schedule
hypotheses_results.explanation: Satisfaction increased from 6.2 to 8.1 on a 10-point scale, a 30.6% improvement. Highly statistically significant with a large effect size.

hypotheses_results.hypothesis: H3: Sick days decrease under the 4-day work week
hypotheses_results.explanation: Sick days decreased from 1.8 to 1.2 per month, a 33% reduction. Paired t-test on monthly data confirms statistical significance.
